prelude:
1. e4 c5 2. d4 cxd4 3. c3 dxc3 4. Nxc3 Nc6 5. Nf3 e6 6. Bc4 Qc7 7. O-O Nf6 8.
Qe2 Ng4 *

solution:
5. Qd5 5... Bb7 ( 5... Bb4+
{ is best. White is slightly better after } 6. Bd2 Nxd2 7. Nbxd2 Nc6 { . } ) 6.
Qxb7 6... Nc6 7. Nd4
{ A defence that was not possible in the previous game (the knight was still on g8 there; analogous to g1 here). }
( 7. Qa6 $2
{ loses the queen in a way we also saw in Game 375, Roberts-Frost (see 6...Qa3 7. Bb5+!): }
7... Bb4+ $1 8. Bd2 Nc5 9. Qb5 Bxd2+ 10. Nbxd2 a6 { . } ) 7... Bb4+
 ( 7... Nxd4 8. Qxe4 { is the point. } ) 8. Nc3 
8... Nxc3 9. Nxc6 { Hitting the bishop. } 9... dxc6 10. Qxc6+
Kf8 11. Bd2 11... Nxa2 12. Rd1 *
prelude: 1. d4 Nf6 2. c4 e5 3. dxe5 Ne4 4. Nf3 b6 *

solution:
{ Mix } 1. Rxg6+ ( 1. Bf2 { was played in the game. } ) 1... Rxg6 2. e6 Kh7 3.
e7 Rg8 4. Kf6
{ On its way to f7 to support the e-pawn, and shouldering the black king. }
4... Rg6+ 5. Kf7 Rg7+ 6. Ke6 Rg8 7. Kd7 Rg7 8. Kd8 *

{ 49. Be4, yes or no? Answer: Yes! } 1. Be4 ( 1. Qd7
{ was played in the game. } ) 1... Qxb1+ 2. Kh2
{ Chess is not checkers! Capturing is not mandatory! } ( 2. Bxb1 d1=Q+ 3. Kh2
Kg8 4. Qc8+ { and White can save herself by giving a perpetual. } ) 2... Qg1+ (
2... Qxe4 3. Qc8+ { , mating. } ) ( 2... Qb8 3. Qxh7# ) ( 2... Kg8 3. Bd5+ Kh8
4. Qc8+ Bf8 5. Qxf8# ) 3. Kxg1 d1=Q+ 4. Kh2 Kg8 5. Bd5+ Qxd5 ( 5... Kh8 6. Qc8+
Bf8 7. Qxf8# ) 6. Qxd5+ Kf8 ( 6... Kh8 7. Qd8+ Bf8 8. Qxf8# ) 7. Qd6+ ( { Or }
7. Qc5+ { . } ) *
